title: Market Profile day-type frequencies: trending days only ~9.5% of all days
claim: Under Market Profile classification, true trending structure (Trend Day + Double-Distribution Trend Day) occurs on about 9.54% of days; the rest are non-trending (Normal 2.43%, Normal Variation 41.77%, Neutral 30.21%, Non-trend 6.81%). A day-type filter must therefore be built to tolerate ~90% non-trend days.

source.label: TrendDayTrading.com — Market Profile Trading: Theory, Analysis and Practice
source.quote: Normal days ... 2.43% of the time ... [Normal Variation] 41.77% of the time ... [Neutral Day] 30.21% of the time ... [Non-trend Day] 6.81% ... Trend days are marked by a long skinny profile.

rule_formal: Trend day (structural): one-timeframing (consecutive 30-min periods making higher highs+higher lows, or inverse) sustained open-to-close; long thin profile. Frequencies: P(trend-type day) = 9.54%; P(neutral) = 30.21%; P(normal variation) = 41.77%. No realtime numeric trigger given on this page — classification is end-of-day structural.

numbers.effect: Trending days 9.54% of all days; Normal 2.43%, Normal Variation 41.77%, Neutral 30.21%, Non-trend 6.81%
numbers.sample: population/instrument/period of the frequency study not stated on page
numbers.market: unstated (Market Profile literature is CME futures-derived)
numbers.caveats: classic MP frequencies circulate without primary attribution; page gives no instrument or years; classification is post-hoc (EOD) — realtime proxy (one-timeframing count, IB extension) must be constructed separately

mechanism: Trend day = 'other-timeframe' participants in control from the open, continuously pulling in new participants; non-trend/neutral days = both sides near agreement on value, responsive trade dominates.
relevance: Q2 direct (day-type taxonomy + base rates; realtime cue = one-timeframing on 30-min periods, countable before noon), Q3 (base rate: EXPAND days are structurally rare ~10%, so a filter that kills them kills the whole edge)

test_design.lookahead_risk: day-type label itself is EOD (lookahead); one-timeframing count at hour N is computable realtime from M1 — test must use the realtime proxy, not the label

overlap_risk: one-timeframing/IB-extension proxy likely correlates with range expansion (falsified); base-rate framing itself has no overlap
